rulings.Case Title: IN RE: THE JAMES B. MCCLATCHY TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 15, 1974
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer Of Jean Coblentz And Andrew Coblentz As Co-Trustees Of The Coblentz 1990 Revocable Trust And Philip Shatz As Executor Of The Estate Of William Ellery Mcclatchy To First Amendment To Amended And Supplemental Petition And Complaint For Declaratory Relief, Breach Of Trust, Aiding And Abetting Breach Of Trust, And Fraud
rulings.Rulings: The demurrer brought by Jean and Andrew Coblentz as Trustees of the 1990 Coblentz Trust and Phillip Shatz as Executor of the Estate of Ellery McClatchy is SUSTAINED in part and OVERRULED in part. With respect to Carlos McClatchy's claims against Jean and Andrew Coblentz as Trustees of the 1990 Coblentz Trust, for which he seeks to hold the Coblentz Trust directly liable, those claims are barred by the one-year statute of limitations set forth in C.C.P. section 366.2. The Court finds that former Probate Code section 21308 did not toll the running of the one-year statute of limitations. That section refers to motions, petitions, or other acts "referred to in subdivision (a) of Section 21320." Former Probate Code section 21320(a) refers specifically to a contest to determine whether a proposed petition "would be a contest" within the terms of a no contest clause in "an instrument containing a no contest clause." It makes no reference to determinations about whether, in the first instance, the instrument contains such a clause or whether a particular clause is a no contest clause. Here, the Court determined that the instrument did not contain a no contest clause for purposes of section 21320(a). Therefore, the safe harbor petition did not toll the statute of limitations by virtue of section 21308. Moreover, the filing of the Petition for Relief from Breach of Trust in September 2012 cannot relate back to the filing of the safe harbor petition that sought entirely different relief. Carlos thus failed to file an action within one year as required by section 366.2 and failed to file a creditor's claim as required by Probate Code section 9000 et seq. within one year of William Coblentz's death, which is a prerequisite to maintaining an action against his estate. (Wood v. Brown (1974) 39 Cal.App.3d 232, 238; Dacey v. Taraday (2011) 196 Cal.App.4th 962, 976-977.) The Coblentz Trustees' demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to those claims. With respect to Carlos's distinct claims against "the Estate of William Coblentz," for which he seeks to recover against Coblentz's insurance policy, i.e., the claims brought pursuant to Probate Code section 550 et seq., those claims are timely under Probate Code sections 551 and 16460(a)(1), as the Petition for Relief from Breach of Trust was filed on September 19, 2012. That is well within one year after "the statute of limitations otherwise applicable" without regard to section 366.2-the time limit set by section 551. The First Amendment and the FAC relate back to the Petition for Relief from Breach of Trust. The Coblentz Trustees' demurrer is thus overruled as to the claims brought pursuant to Probate Code section 550 et seq. Carlos's time to serve the "person designated in writing by the insurer, or, if none, on the insurer" with summons has not yet elapsed, and that service need not be pleaded in order to survive a demurrer. As set forth in Probate Code section 554, however, any judgment in Carlos's favor would be enforceable only to the extent of the insurance coverage and limits and not against property in the Coblentz Trust. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling must so advise all other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:30 p.m. on Wednesday, April 22, 2015. Counsel are directed to submit a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling. [Please see other entry for the balance of the tentative ruling regarding Phillip Shatz's portion of the demurrer.]

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: THE JAMES B. MCCLATCHY TRUST DATED NOVEMBER 15, 1974
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Continuation Of Demurrer Brought By The Coblentz Trustees And Phillip Schatz As Executor Of The Estate Of Ellery Mcclatchy
rulings.Rulings: Phillip Shatz's demurrer is overruled as to both the claims brought directly against Ellery McClatchy's Estate and the claims brought pursuant to Probate Code section 550 et seq. Ellery McClatchy died on September 20, 2011. The Petition for Relief from Breach of Trust, which named Phillip Shatz as the executor of the Estate of Ellery McClatchy and successor in interest to Ellery McClatchy, was filed on September 19, 2012 and thus falls within the one-year statute of limitations under C.C.P. section 366.2. Carlos has pleaded filing of a timely claim in the New York probate proceedings. The parties have not cited, and the Court has not found, any law suggesting that the opening of ancillary administration in California is necessary or appropriate merely to receive a claim against the estate where, as here, a decedent domiciled in New York, who has not left property in California, has an ongoing probate in New York. Ellery McClatchy left no local property that a California court could marshal for the benefit of any local creditor. Mr. Shatz has not convinced the Court on this demurrer that Carlos's submission of a creditor's claim in the New York proceedings, as alleged in the First Amendment, is invalid or insufficient to satisfy the claims procedure of Probate Code section 9000 et seq. The claims brought pursuant to Probate Code section 550 et seq. are also timely under Probate Code sections 551 and 16460(a)(1), and, as with Coblentz, Carlos's time to serve the insurer or person designated by the insurer with summons has not yet elapsed. Mr. Shatz, by making a general appearance in his representative capacity in the action on April 26, 2012, waived the lack of formal substitution under C.C.P. section 377.41. (Wood v. Brown (1974) 39 Cal.App.3d 232, 234, fn. 2.) Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling must so advise all other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:30 p.m. on Wednesday, April 22, 2015. Counsel are directed to submit a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling. [Please see other entry for the portion of the tentative regarding the Coblentz Trustees.]

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Motion To Strike And Demurrer; Etc.
rulings.Rulings: Petitioner's motion to strike the allegations regarding the 1995 deed is denied because the language of the 1995 Deed creates a rebuttable presumption of a joint tenancy. Petitioner's motion to strike the allegations regarding the Deed of Trust taken out by Decedent on march 8, 2004 is granted because Respondent offers no factual support for the allegation that Decedent took out the Deed of Trust in the family home in the amount of $140,000 for the purpose of buying out his siblings' interests in the family home via the 2004 Deed. Petitioner's motion to strike the Reservation Clause is granted because it is unnecessary to reserve future affirmative defenses before they are timely. Petitioner's demurrer as to the first affirmative defense is sustained with leave to amend because Respondent offers no facts specifically attacking each cause of action. Petitioner's demurrer as to the second affirmative defense (standing to sue0 is overruled because Respondent pled sufficient facts establishing this defense. Petitioner's demurrer as to the third affirmative defense (estoppel, waiver, and consent) is sustained with leave to amend because Respondent improperly combined separate defenses into one defense. Petitioner's demurrer as tot he fourth affirmative defense (unclean hands) is sustained with leave to amend because Respondent has not pled facts showing that Respondent engaged in wrongful conduct. Petitioner's demurrer as to the fifth affirmative defense (failure to mitigate) is sustained with leave to amend because Respondent has not pled any facts showing how Petitioner could have reasonably mitigated his damages. Petitioner's demurrer as to the sixth affirmative defense (misrepresentation) should be sustained with leave to amend because Respondent has failed to plead facts establishing several of the elements of misrepresentation. Petitioner's demurrer as to the seventh affirmative defense (laches) is overruled because Respondent has pled sufficient facts establishing this defense. Petitioner's demurrer as to the eighth affirmative defense is sustained without leave to amend because it is unnecessary to reserve future affirmative defenses before they are timely. Respondent's counsel shall prepare the proposed order. Any party choosing to contest the tentative shall notify the Court and opposing counsel no later than 4:00 p.m. on 4/22/2015.
